content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jacquelyn Kitchen 
Development Services Director 
November 6, 2018 
City of Bakersfield - Development Services Dept. 
1 715 Chester A venue 
Bakersfield, CA 93301 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-212 
Dear Ms. Kitchen: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As the Director of the Development Services Department, does the Act prohibit you from 
taking part in governmental decisions involving planning and land development applications and 
permits, when the subject of the application/permit includes a project in which the civil engineering 
firm where your husband is employed provides technical services to the project proponent? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to planning and 
development land applications where the firm that employs your husband provides services to the 
project proponent because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your source of income. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-18-212 
Page No. 2 
You are the Director of the Development Services Department for the City of Bakersfield. 
You report directly to the City Manager, the chief executive officer of the City, and who, in tum, 
reports to the City Council. 
As Director, you are the head of a Department that includes more than 60 full time 
employees who provide the following services to the public: 
Planning Division: Staff processes applications for Land Use entitlements (General Plan 
Amendments, Zone Changes, Site Plan Review, Conditional Use Permits, etc.), maintains 
the City's General Plan, meets with property owners to provide development assistance, 
conducts long-range planning, administrators of the Board of Zoning Adjustment and 
Planning Commission, etc. 
Economic & Community Development Unit. Staff administers federal grant programs from 
the Department of Housing and Urban Development; including receipt and review of 
applications for funds, administers programs, ensures adherence to regulations, conducts 
Economic Development activities, etc. 
Building Division: Staff processes applications for grading and building permits from the 
public, conducts technical review of plans, conducts field inspections for construction 
projects, and provides Code Enforcement services. 
In your role as Department Head, your responsibilities include, but are not limited to: 
• 
Reporting to the City Manager, and providing consultation to other Department Heads, 
Planning Commissioners, City Council members and other elected officials. 
• 
Providing supervision to five "Direct-Report" management-level employees within the 
Department (Planning Director, Building Director, Community Development Coordinator, 
Economic Development Principal Planner, and Business Manager). 
• 
Providing secondary supervision and guidance to other Department staff. 
• 
Assisting the public in navigating the planning and building process. 
• 
Meeting with developers to answer questions regarding the local development process. 
• 
Making decisions that involve ministerial acts (administrative approvals). 
• 
Providing recommendations on discretionary applications that are considered by City 
Council. 


The Firm 
File No. A-18-212 
Page No. 3 
Yoµr husband is employed as a "Principal Planner/ Project Manager" with QK, Inc., a civil 
engineering firm that has office locations throughout California, including one in Bakersfield. He is 
a salaried employee who reports to a group leader, who reports to a Branch Manager. The Branch 
Manager reports to senior Management at the Corporate Office, which is located in another city. 
The Firm is an incorporated entity. The Firm offers a variety of technical services in the areas of 
engineering, planning and environmental services. The Firm provides these services in two 
capacities that are relevant to this discussion: 
The Firm provides services to private-sector clients wishing to pursue development projects. 
These services include, but are not limited to: preparation of subdivision maps, engineering surveys 
and reports, and varioµs environmental technical reports related to biology, water, land use, urban 
decay, etc. In some jurisdictions, the Firm may also submit various development applications to the 
local jurisdictions on behalf of their clients. Examples of these applications include Site Plan 
Review, General Plan Amendments, Zone Changes, grading/building permits, etc. In a telephone 
conversation, you noted that the Firm does not typically submit applications to your department, 
rather, the project proponent or property owner submits them. Also, you have indicated that the 
Firm is normally not named in the application. 
The Firm also provides services to public-sector clients, such as Cities and Counties. This 
typically occurs in one of two forms: (1) The Firm acts as "contract" staff for the jurisdiction (i.e., 
Acting City Engineer, but not in the case of Bakersfield), or (2) The Firm prepares survey and 
engineering work and biological monitoring services for public projects. 
ANALYSIS 
' 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. 
Section 87103 defines financial interests to include: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more." (Sections 87103(a).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution 
made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised 
to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 


File No. A-18-212 
Page No. 4 
• 
Your personal finances, including those of your immediate family. (Section 
87103.) 
Assuming you meet the financial standards set forth above, you have a community property 
interest in your spouse's income from the Firm. Therefore, you have an economic interest in the 
Firm as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).). Next, we determine if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that a decision will have a material financial effect on your financial interest. 
Reasonably Foreseeable and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) states that a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable where 
the financial interest is explicitly involved as a named party or subject of the decisions. A financial 
interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the issuance or approval of an 
entitlement to the financial interest. (Regulation 18701(a).) As to materiality, Regulation 18702.l(a) 
states that a financial effect is material when the business entity initiates the proceeding in which 
the governmental decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other 
government action concerning the business entity. 
Where the Firm has filed the application for an entitlement on behalf of a client, the Firm is 
explicitly involved, and it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision on the application will have a 
material financial effect on your husband's firm, a source of income. 
Regulation 18701 (b) sets out the appropriate standard to determine foreseeability where the 
financial interest is not explicitly involved in the decision. Generally, a financial effect is 
reasonably foreseeable where it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical ... absent extraordinary circumstances beyond the public official's 
control." (Regulation 1870l(b).) A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable. (Id.) The standard to determine materiality, for a business entity as source of income, 
including salary, is set forth in Regulation 18702.1 (b) (business entity, not explicitly involved). 
Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change ... in the value of a 
privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.l(b).) 
Examples of decision that may contribute to a change in the value of a business include 
decisions that authorize or establish conditions for an activity in which the business is engaged, 
change the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is engaged, or change the 
need for the products or services that the business entity supplies. (Regulation 18702. l(b)(l) 
through (3).) 
You note that the Firm has an office located in Bakersfield, and that it has a history of 
providing services to clients located within the City. The Firm provides engineering work necessary 
for a client to file an application with your department, and for development work. Although the 
Firm does not actually file the application, it does have an interest in the approval of applications 
where it has provided engineering and related services for the client. Moreover, the approval of an 
application authorizes work in which the Firm is engaged, may result in additional work for the 


File No. A-18-212 
Page No. 5 
client related to the application, and may change the demand for the Firm's service in recognition of 
the Firm's ability to achieve results on behalf of its clients. As such, a prudent person with 
sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable these decisions may contribute to a 
change in the value of the Firm. Therefore, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions 
relating to applications where the firm that employs your husband provides services to the project 
proponent because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
your source of income. 
Influencing a decision 
As a public official, you must not make, participate in making, or attempt to influence a 
decision in regards to an application before your agency, the Development Services Department, if 
it would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon your interests. You must also 
not use your official position to attempt to influence a decision by contacting or appearing before 
any agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of the Development Services Department. 
Regulation 18704 defines "making, participating in making, or using his or her official 
position to influence a governmental decision" as follows: 
(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental decision if the 
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or 
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual 
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental 
decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for 
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive 
review. 
(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public official 
uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision ifhe or she: 
(1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency 
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose 
of affecting a decision; or 
(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the 
purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act 
within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact. 
Meet or Communicate with City Staff: 
Your "agency" includes staff assigned to the Development Services Department as well as 
City staff that may be shared with the Development Services Department. (See Farrell Advice 
Letter, No. I-03-121.) Shared city staff are considered to simultaneously represent each of the 
multiple agencies they work under during a review process. If a City staff member is shared with 


File No. A-18-212 
Page No. 6 
your agency, the Development Services Department, you are prohibited from attempting to 
influence the shared staff member regarding a decision that is before the Development Services 
Department. You may otherwise communicate with non-shared City staff. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
Q;ha:] 
Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
As the Director of the Development Services Department, does the Act prohibit you from 
taking part in governmental decisions involving planning and land development applications and 
permits, when the subject of the application/permit includes a project in which the civil engineering 
firm where your husband is employed provides technical services to the project proponent?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to planning and 
development land applications where the firm that employs your husband provides services to the 
project proponent because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your source of income.

taking part in governmental decisions involving planning and land development applications and 
permits, when the subject of the application/permit includes a project in which the civil engineering 
firm where your husband is employed provides technical services to the project proponent?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to planning and 
development land applications where the firm that employs your husband provides services to the 
project proponent because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on your source of income.
